strap: A daily dispatch from the world you are entering

lead.title: The saleroom rebounds, and the family collection quietly becomes a credit line
lead.standfirst: Christie's and Sotheby's have posted their strongest first half in years, and the most consequential detail sits not in the hammer prices but in the lending books built around them.
lead.body: Both houses published half year figures on 15 July. Christie's reported revenue of USD 4.5 billion, with public auction sales up 71 per cent year on year at USD 3.5 billion and a sell-through rate of 91 per cent by lot, against 87 per cent in 2025. Sotheby's public auction sales reached USD 3.4 billion, up 59 per cent, with total turnover of USD 4.4 billion including a record USD 826 million in private sales, an all-time high for the house. The single largest collection sold so far this year was that of the publisher S. I. Newhouse, which totalled USD 630.8 million in New York in May and produced two of the year's three highest prices, Jackson Pollock's Number 7A at USD 181.2 million and Constantin Brancusi's Danaide at USD 107.6 million. In June the Lewis collection made USD 406.2 million in London, the highest value sale of Impressionist, Modern and contemporary art ever staged in Europe. The recovery has been carried by trophy material and by what the trade calls, without much affection, the luxury genre: watches, jewellery, cars, handbags, memorabilia. At Christie's that category alone reached USD 539 million, up 15 per cent, and the collection of the businessman Jim Irsay made USD 105.2 million across five auctions closing on 6 July, the highest total ever recorded for a memorabilia collection. Both houses describe luxury as the entry point for a younger client. Christie's says 47 per cent of its new clients this year are Millennial or Generation Z, and that 85 per cent of bids came in online. The structural story is financing. Sotheby's completed a USD 900 million securitisation in January, the ArtFi Master Trust Series 2026-1, bundling art-backed loans into tradable notes sold to institutional investors. For the first time the issuance also included loans secured against collectible cars. Christie's, which opened its art financing arm five years ago, now lends against cars, handbags, watches and increasingly wine. Its chief executive is explicit that many borrowers are not short of capital, they are simply optimising how they use it, and frequently reinvest the proceeds in more art. For the household this changes what a collection is. A picture on a wall that also secures a loan is no longer only an object to be insured, hung and dusted. It is a financial instrument with covenants attached, and its condition, location, provenance file and valuation history acquire a second life inside the family's balance sheet. Loan agreements can restrict where a work may travel, who may handle it and how it must be stored. The person who signs for the crate is now, in practice, part of a credit arrangement.
lead.why: When a collection is pledged as collateral, the accuracy of the house inventory, condition reports and loan documentation stops being an administrative nicety and becomes a financial control. Whoever manages the residence needs to know which works are encumbered before agreeing to move one.

articles.title: The estate manager's pay keeps climbing, because almost nobody holds all three skills at once
articles.body:
The 2026 salary guides published by the private staffing agencies converge on a wide and rising band. In the United States estate managers are quoted from USD 85,000 to USD 250,000, with experienced candidates on base salaries of USD 150,000 to USD 200,000. By market, New York runs from USD 120,000 to USD 180,000, the Hamptons from USD 130,000 to USD 190,000 and Palm Beach from USD 110,000 to USD 160,000. In the United Kingdom the equivalent posts are quoted from GBP 110,000 to GBP 220,000 and above, with the spread driven by the scale of the estate, the headcount reporting in, the size of the budget under authority and whether the role covers one property or several.

The demand side explains the drift upward. Agencies report estate manager searches growing about 16 per cent year on year, and estate management now accounts for roughly one in ten client requests in the United States. The driver is portfolio ownership: families are acquiring additional residences across jurisdictions, and each new address adds a payroll, a contractor list, a security posture and a compliance burden that somebody has to hold.

The constraint is not enthusiasm but the shape of the profile. The brief that agencies are asked to fill combines three competences that rarely sit in one person. First, security literacy, meaning the ability to specify and supervise a protection detail, a surveillance system and a travel protocol without outsourcing judgement entirely. Second, technical command of building systems, from plant rooms and pool infrastructure to networks, access control and increasingly the automation layer that ties them together. Third, genuine management of people, budgets and vendors across multiple sites and time zones.

Most candidates arrive strong in one of the three and passable in a second. The market is therefore paying a premium for the rare third leg rather than for seniority as such, which is why two candidates with identical years of service can be quoted seventy thousand dollars apart. For anyone building a career in private service, the practical implication is that breadth now compounds faster than tenure.
articles.why: The pay gap in this market is a skills gap, not a seniority gap. Deliberately acquiring the weakest of the three competences, usually the technical or the security side, is the most direct route to the top of the published band.
articles.src: Morgan & Mallet International, household staff salary guide 2026

articles.title: The family office learns to write down what it pays, and the first hire after a liquidity event is settled
articles.body:
A programme convened by the law firm Katten on 6 May, with Botoff Consulting and the search firm Mack International, set out where family office pay is heading in the second half of 2026. Total direct compensation for both executives and staff has risen meaningfully year on year. The discretionary bonus is giving way to structured annual incentive plans built on individual performance, organisational performance and investment performance, in that order, a sequence that matters because it signals that portfolio return is no longer accepted as the sole measure of a professional's value.

On the supply side, Mack International describes a large uptick in activity, driven by newly created offices and by a wave of succession as principals from the baby boomer generation step back. Experienced talent is not keeping pace. The chief financial officer is currently the hardest seat to fill, because strategic and tax-oriented professionals with estate planning experience are exceptionally scarce, and more than a third of offices in Botoff's 2026 compensation and talent planning survey reported recruiting difficulty in the past year, with the largest offices reporting the most.

The liveliest exchange concerned a practical question: after a liquidity event, who should the family hire first. The recommendation was a family office leader or a chief financial officer, and specifically not a chief investment officer, since a CIO accustomed to reporting directly to the family creates friction when a chief executive is later placed above them. Bringing across a trusted advisor from the sold operating company was flagged as a common source of dysfunction within a couple of years, because running a business and serving as a professional manager to a principal are different disciplines. Build the scaffolding, the panel argued, before pursuing investment hires.

The era of the wink and a nod arrangement is closing. Professionals recruited from institutional backgrounds now expect transparency, documentation and a stated compensation philosophy, and families are increasingly recruiting directly from other family offices. Compensation represents the dominant share of family office operating cost, and the guidance is to re-examine pay structures every two to three years and incentive plans annually.
articles.why: The same formalisation is arriving in the household. Where the family office writes down its compensation philosophy, the house eventually writes down its job descriptions, review cycles and bonus criteria, and vague arrangements become harder to defend.

articles.title: The order book thins for a second year, and Italy now builds more than half the fleet
articles.body:
The 2026 Global Order Book compiled by BOAT International records 1,093 yachts of 24 metres and above on order or in build, down from 1,138 in the 2025 edition. It is the second consecutive annual decline by unit count, and it is not a story of weakening demand so much as of consolidation: fewer hulls, bigger boats, longer waits.

Italy's dominance has hardened. Italian yards account for 568 units, roughly 52 per cent of the entire order book. The Azimut Benetti group leads the rankings for a twenty-sixth consecutive year, with Sanlorenzo, including its sister brand Nautor Swan, in second place at 130 yachts and 4,698 metres of combined length. The concentration means that a large share of the world's private fleet is now being built within a few hundred kilometres of the Tyrrhenian and Adriatic coasts, with the supply chain, the refit capacity and the specialist labour clustered accordingly.

The practical consequence is scheduling. Most established yards cannot deliver a new build before 2028 at the earliest, and at the top-tier German and Dutch builders the wait extends three to four years. A first-time owner entering the market in 2026 is therefore not choosing between yards so much as between three routes: buy an existing hull on the brokerage market and take delivery now, take a slot at a mid-tier yard with available capacity, or wait several seasons for the yard actually wanted.

For the shore side of a household this reshapes the calendar. A build slot secured today commits the family, and the staff who serve it, to a specification frozen years before the boat floats. Interiors, crew accommodation, tender arrangements and the stowage that determines whether a season runs smoothly are decided at a point when the family's travel pattern may look quite different from the one it will have on delivery.
articles.why: Long lead times move the useful conversation forward by years. The person who will run the guest programme has more influence at the specification stage than at any point afterwards, and that stage is now happening well before delivery.

articles.title: The relocation is decided by the tax adviser and executed by the school registrar
articles.body:
Dubai's private school system now exceeds 210 institutions across six major curricula, with annual fees running from about AED 30,000 to AED 120,000. Several new campuses open for the 2026 to 2027 academic year, among them Harrow International School Dubai, and existing schools are expanding capacity. The build-out is a direct response to arrivals: the emirate has absorbed a sustained inflow of internationally mobile families, accelerated in the British case by the ending of the non-domicile regime in April 2025.

The sequence of a family relocation is rarely the one described in the press. The tax position may be settled in a single meeting, and the property acquired within a season. The schooling is what actually determines the date of the move, because places in the sought-after schools are finite, admission cycles are fixed, and a family with three children of different ages frequently cannot place them all in one institution in the same year. Assessments, entrance testing and waiting lists compress the window, and the academic calendar, not the fiscal one, sets the deadline.

This is why private tutors are engaged so early in the process. Families moving from one curriculum to another, typically English national curriculum to International Baccalaureate or an American programme, use tutoring to bridge the gap before assessment rather than after arrival, and in some cases a tutor travels with the family for a transitional year while places are secured. The tutor becomes, briefly, the most operationally significant member of the household.

Dubai's wider private infrastructure has matured in step. The city now supports a genuine ecosystem of members' clubs rather than the handful of hotel-adjacent rooms that existed a few years ago, which matters because a family relocating without an existing social network needs somewhere to reconstruct one.
articles.why: In a relocation the household staff usually own the school logistics: assessment dates, uniform lists, transport, medical records, transitional tutoring. Getting the academic calendar into the family's planning early is one of the most visible contributions the role makes.

articles.title: On Paraga Beach, a Parisian house tries the Cyclades
articles.body:
Fouquet's has opened on the southern coast of Mykonos at Paraga Beach, taking the French group beyond its Paris and Riviera base. The property runs to 61 suites and three villas, built in a contemporary reading of Cycladic architecture, with indoor and outdoor pools, a spa operated in partnership with Dr Barbara Sturm and a basketball court cut directly into the rock.

The villa component is the part worth noting. On an island where the private rental market has long outcompeted hotels for the largest parties, a branded house is selling the combination that families increasingly want: the privacy and space of a villa with the service infrastructure, kitchen and security of a hotel behind it. That combination has become the standard brief for a summer in the Aegean, and it is why so much of the new inventory across the Cyclades is being built as suites with pools rather than conventional rooms.

For anyone arranging a Mediterranean summer, the operational point is that inventory of this kind is thin and moves early. First-release rates on 2026 debuts have gone quickly, and the multi-bedroom units are typically committed well before the season opens.
articles.why: Knowing which properties can absorb a whole family under one roof, with a private pool and a kitchen that can be used, saves a great deal of negotiation later. The distinction between a suite and a genuinely private house is the one the principal will notice.

briefs.t: Art-backed lending is institutionalised, and now includes cars
briefs.b: Sotheby's completed a USD 900 million securitisation in January, the ArtFi Master Trust Series 2026-1, which the house describes as significantly oversubscribed. It bundles art-backed loans into tradable notes sold to institutional investors, and for the first time included loans secured against collectible cars. The house frames the investor appetite as evidence that art is settling into mainstream wealth management rather than sitting beside it.

briefs.t: The average watch bidder now spends USD 129,000
briefs.b: Average spend per watch bidder at Sotheby's is up around 60 per cent year on year, at USD 129,000, and Millennials and Generation Z now account for roughly one third of watch bidders. The house reports the demand shifting toward independent makers such as F. P. Journe, Simon Brette and Rexhep Rexhepi, and describes the buying as less speculative than in the pre-pandemic period.

briefs.t: Seventy per cent of family offices now invest directly
briefs.b: Roughly 70 per cent of family offices make direct private investments, and 83 per cent of those deals are structured as co-investments rather than solo commitments. The pattern explains a good deal of the recruitment pressure on investment and finance staff, since direct dealing requires diligence capacity that an outsourced model does not.

briefs.t: A projected 165,000 millionaires will move country this year
briefs.b: Henley & Partners projects 165,000 high-net-worth individuals will relocate internationally during 2026, a 16 per cent increase on the record 142,000 moves recorded in 2025. Singapore, Italy, Switzerland, Greece, Hong Kong and New Zealand are named among the strongest destinations, while the United Kingdom, Germany, France, Norway and South Korea face growing competitive pressure.
